retrieved_docs.title: Bispecific Antibody Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Blinatumomab (2014): For CD19-positive B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia therapy Emicizumab (2017): For reducing bleeding frequency in Hemophilia A with factor VIII inhibitors Amivantamab (2021): For treating locally advanced or metastatic non–small cell lung cancer with epidermal growth factor receptor exon-20 insertion mutation Faricimab (2022): For therapy of neovascular age-related macular degeneration and diabetic macular edema Teclistamab (2022): For relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma treatment Mosunetuzumab (2022): For relapsed or refractory follicular lymphoma therapy Epcoritamab and glofitamab (2023):  For treating relapsed or refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma Talquetamab and elranatamab (2023): For therapy of relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma

retrieved_docs.title: Sezary Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Targeted agents approved for mycosis fungoides/Sezary syndrome include brentuximab vedotin (BV) and mogamulizumab. BV is an anti-CD30 monoclonal antibody conjugated with the tubulin inhibitor monomethylauristatin E approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for patients who have received prior therapy; its approval is based on the ALCANZA trial. [14] Moga is a defucosylated humanized antibody directed against the chemokine receptor CCR4 overexpressed on malignant T-cells, and its approval is based on the MAVORIC trial. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Second Relapse or Subsequent Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: In 2022, the FDA approved teclistamab, the first bispecific T-cell engager antibody against CD38 expressed on the myeloma cells, for patients with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma who have failed 4 prior lines of therapy. Belantamab mafodotin is anti-BCMA humanized immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibody conjugated with monomethyl auristatin. The drug received accelerated approval in the US for patients who had progressed on at least 4 lines of therapy. It is being withdrawn from the market based on the results of randomized phase 3 trials (DREAMM-3), as it did not show clinical benefit compared to pomalidomide and dexamethasone.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Bispecific antibodies (bsAbs) have shown promising results in early clinical trials. A bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) enhances the interaction of T-cells and MM cells by targeting BCMA on MM cells and CD3 receptors on the T-cells causing tumor lysis. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The main areas described in the literature for the development of bsmAbs include inhibition of multiple cell surface receptors, multiple ligand blockade, receptor cross-linkage, and recruitment of Fc receptor absent T-cells (which would normally not be active with antibody stimulation due to the absence of Fc receptors. The design and implementation of bsmAbs have proven to be challenging despite the conceptually sound nature of the propositions. For example, catumaxomab was one such agent that was initially approved for malignant ascites (2009) but withdrawn from the market in 2017 despite its success due to adverse events. The mechanism of action has been described as trifunctional. There are two antigen-binding regions and an Fc region present on the molecule. The first antigen-binding region targets a trans-membrane glycoprotein named EpCAM, which can be found on malignant cells. The second antigen-binding region binds CD3 antigens on T-cells of the immune system. The Fc region functions to activate and recruit immune cells, including macrophages and natural killer cells, to act against malignant cells. Overall, catumaxomab activates multiple pathways for immune-mediated cell destruction (i.e., ADCC, ADCP, etc.) and cytokine-mediated cytotoxicity. [11] [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: T-Cell Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment regimens now under phase III are  Brentuximab Vedotin (anti CD30 monoclonal antibody) [10] and CHP vs. CHOP,  Romidepsin (HDAC inhibitor) CHOP (Ro-CHOP) Versus CHOP, these two for induction and Pralatrexate Versus Observation Following CHOP-based Chemotherapy for maintenance. In ALCL, CD 30 expression is assessed; if positive, the treatment regimen brentuximab vedotin, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and prednisone (BV +CHP), is considered superior to CHOP.ALK positivity in ALCL also plays an important role as ALK inhibitors(Crizotinib, Alectinib). Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma is generally refractory to CHOP regimens because of the presence of the P-glycoprotein gene. For localized variants, chemoradiotherapy using dexamethasone, carboplatin, ifosfamide, and etoposide, whereas for advanced disease, L-asparaginase with ifosfamide, etoposide, dexamethasone, and methotrexate (SMILE). Adult T-cell lymphoma-leukemia has a very poor prognosis. Because of the rapid progression of the disease, the most appropriate treatment modality is unclear, and the patient should be enrolled in a clinical trial. The current treatment modality is VCAP (vincristine, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and prednisone), AMP (doxorubicin, ranimustine, and prednisone), VECP (vindesine, etoposide, carboplatin, prednisone), and intrathecal chemotherapy with the addition of anti-CCR4 antibody mogamulizumab. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with severe disease who continue demonstrating response at 24 weeks to teprotumumab can be considered for additional infusions using the same dosing schedule. [56] In contrast, early discontinuation should be considered if there is no improvement at the 4th to 6th infusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Anifrolumab  -- Administration -- Specific Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding women: No information is available on the clinical use of anifrolumab during breastfeeding. As anifrolumab is a large protein molecule, the amount in milk is likely to be very low. Anifroluman may be destroyed in the infant's gastrointestinal tract and is probably minimally absorbed. [21] Pediatric patients: No dose adjustment information is available for pediatric patients as no safety and efficacy were evaluated for patients younger than 18.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocrelizumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Ocrelizumab is a second-generation anti-CD20 recombinant monoclonal antibody with a molecular mass of 145kDa. It is a follow-up to the anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody rituximab but with a humanized IgG1 tail. [3] Humanization of the antibody is expected to reduce antigenicity and improve CD20 binding. [4] CD20 is a phosphoprotein expressed on a broad range of human B-cells. However, it is not present in the early lineage (stem cells and pro-B-cells) or late lineage (plasmablasts and plasma cells). [5] Ocrelizumab binds to CD20 on B-lymphocytes leading to antibody-dependent cellular cytolysis and complement-mediated lysis. [6] While the precise method by which ocrelizumab exerts its effect on multiple sclerosis is unknown, it depletes B-cells which are thought to affect the pathogenesis of multiple sclerosis using cytokine regulation, antigen presentation, autoantibody production. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 630
retrieved_docs.content: Tanezumab, abciximab and ibalizumab are all classified within the broader category of monoklonální protilátky (monoclonal antibodies). The relationship “isa” indicates that abciximab is a monoclonal antibody, while “mapped_to” shows that ibalizumab is linked to the monoclonal‑antibody class. The “inverse_isa” relationship denotes that monoklonální protilátky constitute the general class to which tanezumab belongs, confirming that tanezumab is a specific monoclonal antibody. Together, these connections highlight that each of these therapeutic agents shares the fundamental characteristic of being monoclonal antibodies, which is important for understanding their mechanisms of action and clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 751
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is identified as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor, placing it within the product class of selective adhesion molecule inhibitors. In addition, crizanlizumab is classified as both an immunomodulatory agent (imunomodulátory) and an immunosuppressive agent (imunosupresiva), reflecting its dual capacity to modify and dampen immune system activity. These properties align crizanlizumab with the broader disposition of immunomodulators, indicating that its therapeutic action involves regulation of immune responses through targeted inhibition of adhesion molecules. This integrated classification underscores crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune pathways and its potential utility in conditions where controlling immune cell adhesion and activity is clinically beneficial.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 273
retrieved_docs.content: Abciximab and ibalizumab are both classified as monoklonální protilátky (monoclonal antibodies), indicating that each of these agents belongs to the therapeutic class of antibodies derived from a single immune cell clone and designed to target specific antigens. This shared categorization highlights their role in targeted medical treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1602
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is classified within the therapeutic class of Immunological Agents [TC] and is designated as an Immunomodulator (disposition). It is a specific type of Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product), reflecting its mechanism of action that targets adhesion molecules to modulate immune cell interactions. Additionally, crizanlizumab is recognized as an imunosupresiva, indicating its broader role as an immunosuppressive agent. Together, these classifications emphasize crizanlizumab’s function as an immunological drug that modulates immune responses through selective inhibition of adhesion molecules, positioning it within both the immunomodulator and immunosuppressive categories of therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1386
retrieved_docs.content: Abciximab is classified as a monoclonal antibody.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 37
retrieved_docs.content: Carglumic acid, crizanlizumab, and dextrose monohydrate are all classified as agents affecting blood or body fluid. Dextrose monohydrate functions as the active ingredient of the pharmaceutical preparation glucose 100 mg/mL injection, while its anhydrous form, anhydrous dextrose, is identified as the active moiety of the same injection product. Crizanlizumab is placed within the therapeutic class of immunological agents and is specifically described as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor. It is also categorized as an immunomodulator and is recognized for its immunosuppressive properties, linking it to the disposition category of immunomodulators. This collection of relationships clarifies the pharmacologic categorization, formulation roles, and mechanisms of action of these agents, informing their use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 132
retrieved_docs.content: Carglumic acid, crizanlizumab, and dextrose monohydrate are all agents that affect blood or body fluids. Crizanlizumab is further defined as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor and is placed within the therapeutic class of immunological agents. In clinical pharmacology it is regarded as an immunomodulator and also exhibits immunosuppressive activity, reflecting its capacity to alter immune pathways. Carglumic acid shares the broader classification of an agent affecting blood or body fluids, while dextrose monohydrate is likewise categorized in this group. Recognizing these interrelated classifications helps clinicians understand the mechanisms of action and therapeutic contexts of each agent, guiding appropriate patient care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 486
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is categorized within the therapeutic class Immunological Agents [TC] and is described as an Immunomodulator (disposition), indicating that it functions as an immunomodulatory agent. It is identified as a Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product), reflecting its mechanism of targeting adhesion molecules. In addition, crizanlizumab is characterized as both imunomodulátory and imunosupresiva, underscoring its dual role as an immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive medication. This collection of classifications highlights crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune responses through selective adhesion molecule inhibition, which is pertinent to therapeutic strategies for inflammatory and vaso‑occlusive disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 603
retrieved_docs.content: Immunological Agents [TC] is the therapeutic class that includes both deflazacort and crizanlizumab. Within this class, crizanlizumab is identified as an immunomodulator disposition and is specifically described as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor product. The drug is also linked to the terms “imunomodulátory” and “imunosupresiva,” indicating that it possesses immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive properties. Together, these relationships highlight that deflazacort and crizanlizumab belong to the same immunological therapeutic category, while crizanlizumab’s mechanism of action involves modulation of immune responses through adhesion‑molecule inhibition and broader immunosuppressive effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: shared ATC classification, while also possessing distinct mechanistic attributes such as inhibition of selective adhesion molecules or modulation of immune responses. Recognizing both the common therapeutic grouping and the unique pharmacologic actions of each agent supports more integrated patient care and informs clinicians when selecting or comparing treatments within the hematology and immunology domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 800
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are classified as members of the therapeutic class “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema,” and this class is a subclass of the ATC category “other hematological agents.” Heme products (pharmacologic substance) are also defined as a subclass of “other hematological agents.” Crizanlizumab is described as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor and is linked to the disposition “Immunomodulator (disposition),” indicating that it possesses immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive properties (noted by the inverse‑ISA relationships with “imunomodulátory” and “imunosupresiva”). Consequently, crizanlizumab is a member of the “other hematological agents” ATC group. Voxelotor is likewise listed as a member of “other hematological agents.” Together, these entities illustrate the breadth of the ATC “other hematological agents” category, encompassing treatments for hereditary angioedema (ecallantide, icatibant), heme‑related pharmacologic substances, and novel agents such as crizanlizumab that act as selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitors with immunomodulatory effects, all of which are relevant for the management of various hematologic and inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 478
retrieved_docs.content: CP 609754 is a more specific entity that falls under the broader category of technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab, indicating a narrower relationship to this radiolabeled antibody preparation. Technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab itself originates from both monoclonal antibodies (“monoklonální protilátky”) and organotechnetium compounds (“organotechneciové sloučeniny”), reflecting its dual derivation as a labeled biological agent. Within the class of monoclonal antibodies, abciximab is explicitly classified as a member, while ibalizumab is mapped to this same class, underscoring its identity as a monoclonal antibody therapeutic. Tanezumab represents a specific instance of the monoclonal antibody class, illustrating an inverse “is‑a” relationship where the broader category (monoclonal antibodies) encompasses this particular drug. Together, these connections highlight how diagnostic agents such as technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab are built from antibody and technetium chemistry, and how various therapeutic monoclonal antibodies—including abciximab, ibalizumab, and tanezumab—share a common biological framework that informs their clinical use and development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 497
retrieved_docs.content: Carglumic acid, crizanlizumab, and dextrose monohydrate are all classified as agents that affect blood or body fluids. In addition to this broad classification, crizanlizumab is specifically identified as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor product and is recognized for its immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive properties, reflecting its disposition as an immunomodulator. Dextrose monohydrate, as a blood‑or‑body‑fluid agent, is commonly used as a carbohydrate solution for intravenous administration. Together, these agents illustrate the diverse therapeutic categories—ranging from metabolic supplements like carglumic acid and dextrose monohydrate to targeted biologic therapies such as crizanlizumab—under the umbrella of substances that influence the composition or function of blood and other body fluids.
